food_name: Hot and Sour Soup with Meat
aliases: Hot and Sour Soup Non Veg

english: Hot and Sour Soup Non Veg

region_of_origin: China (adapted across Southeast Asia and Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hot and sour soup with meat. FOODON_00002473 (soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest parent. No specific hot-and-sour soup code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to hot and sour soup with meat. No code assigned.

preparation_method: seasoned with vinegar and white pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of hot and sour soup with meat is approximately 300 g, typically served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup, restaurant-style, with meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exactly matches restaurant-style hot and sour soup with meat as served in Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for hot and sour soup (NDB 06416 / FDC ID 172950) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy composite soup data. Protein and fat values reflect the non-vegetarian variant with chicken or pork strips. Sodium reflects restaurant-level seasoning. Values are per 100 g of prepared soup.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed soups and broth-based dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable and meat soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot and sour soup with meat in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous broth-based soups with low starch content (GI range 30–40 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable and meat soups). The thickening starch (cornstarch) contributes modest glycemic load but the overall carbohydrate content per 100 g is low (4.2 g). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 4.2 × 3) / 100 = 4.4 g. GI category: low (≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, broth, and restaurant seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit hot and sour soup intake or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium — more than half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot and sour soup with meat has a low GI (estimated 35) and a glycemic load of approximately 4.4 per standard bowl, making it a suitable starter for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium intake is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from meat and egg components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content in restaurant-style hot and sour soup; choosing lean meat variants and requesting low-sodium broth can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and sour soup with meat (酸辣汤) is a classic Chinese broth-based soup widely served across Chinese restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The non-vegetarian version typically contains strips of chicken or pork, silken tofu, wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and egg ribbons in a savoury chicken broth seasoned with rice vinegar, soy sauce, white pepper, and chilli. The soup is thickened with cornstarch to give it a slightly viscous, silky texture. The flavour profile is a balance of sharp sourness from vinegar, heat from white pepper and chilli, and umami depth from the broth and soy sauce. Per 100 g, the soup provides approximately 38 kcal, 2.8 g protein, 1.2 g fat, and 4.2 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-calorie, low-fat dish. Sodium content is high at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoned broth, which is a key concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g bowl is approximately 4.4, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size and sodium are monitored. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy and egg allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its non-vegetarian form. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Chinese and pan-Asian restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact match for restaurant-style hot and sour soup with meat was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite soup data (NDB 06416 / FDC ID 172950) with adjustments for the non-vegetarian variant. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database broth-soup entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is an approved primary source and the adaptation is well-documented.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for this soup category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for this soup category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for this soup category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — no trans fat reported for this soup type. The food is served at Aryana North Indian Restaurant on South Bridge Road, Singapore, described as a pan-Asian soup of choice; the canonical dish identity is Chinese hot and sour soup with meat, which is the standard preparation matching the menu description. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant context (North Indian) may use pork in the Chinese-style soup variant; halal status cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific verification. GI category is low (GI 35 ≤ 55) per Veritas threshold rules.
